design_philosophy.core_loop: Build and manage a railroad company by laying track, building stations, scheduling trains, and managing finances across multiple time periods
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Economic competition with rival railroad companies using stock dealings and rate wars; balancing infrastructure investment with profitability
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a railroad magnate who builds transcontinental rail networks and outmaneuvers business competitors
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Sid Meier's design philosophy emphasizes emergent player creativity within economic systems. The game provides multiple solution paths (bond financing, competitive sabotage, route optimization) rather than prescriptive objectives. Railroad-building is the means, not just the end—economic viability and business strategy are equally valid win conditions. The game respects player agency in determining what 'success' looks like (pure profit, network coverage, market dominance, etc.)

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player selects starting conditions and maps with varying complexity; can adjust difficulty implicitly through scenario choice (different regions have different economic conditions and competitor aggression). Economic failure is the primary challenge rather than time pressure or pattern execution.

progression.upgrade_system: Technology progression across time periods (new locomotive types, improved track capabilities); access to advanced financing mechanisms and industrial development options unlock as company grows
progression.unlock_mechanism: Time-based (historical progression through centuries); economic milestone-based (profitability unlocks bond access, stock market options)
replayability_hooks: Multiple regional maps with distinct geography and economies (U.S. West, Midwest, Northeast, England, Europe, South America, Africa in Deluxe)
Different starting conditions and competitor configurations per scenario
Open-ended economic sandbox—no single 'winning' strategy enforced
Stock market and business sabotage provide dynamic pressure and emergent narratives

world_building_techniques: Regional map design with historically authentic geography and city placement
Cargo types reflect regional economies (e.g., troops in Africa, sugar in South America)
Dynamic rival railroad companies with named presence create implicit narrative of industrial competition
Time period progression (1830s-2000s in later entries) creates historical context
Economic simulation implicitly tells stories of boom, bust, and industrial consolidation

faction_indicators: Rival railroad companies (named competitors with distinct corporate identities)
Regional economic factions (cities and industries that favor certain rail routes)
Stock market actors (shareholders and speculators who influence company value)
danger_signals: Competitor rate wars that undercut profitability
Stock market attacks where rivals buy company shares to influence direction
Bandits (in Deluxe) who hijack trains—economic sabotage with physical threat
Economic downturns that affect cargo demand and bond interest rates
environmental_storytelling: Industrial development spreads along rail lines—visible economic impact of player choices
Ghost towns and abandoned routes reveal failed business decisions
Competitor railroad networks visible on map show rival strategies and territorial control
Regional specialization emerges through cargo flow patterns (e.g., agricultural regions export grain, mining areas export ore)
